filename: How to use powerlifting bands PTC.pdf
source: How to use powerlifting bands PTC.pdf
strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
2

Powerlifting bands and chains allow you to implement accommodating resistance. This
can help you fit an exercise's resistance curve to your strength curve so that you can
overload more of the range of motion, as opposed to just the sticking point region. It will
also make the exercise easier on your joints. This guide will teach you how implement
bands for the barbell bench press and squat. Alternatively, you can use powerlifting
chains with a similar resistance.

The barbell bench press
There are 2 ways to attach bands: the conventional set-up and the reverse band set-up.

Conventional set-up
During the conventional set-up, the bands should be attached directly below the
shoulder or slightly further down on your body towards the point where the bar touches
your chest. It doesn't matter how you achieve this. You may have to improvise.

3

Here are a few examples of the conventional set-up.

Reverse band set-up

4

During the reverse band set-up, the bands should be attached roughly above the point
where the bar touches your chest/abs or a bit higher up on your body towards your
shoulders. It doesn't matter how you achieve this. You may have to improvise. Since the
bands are now helping you lift the weight instead of resisting you, you will be able to use
far more weight with this set-up than with the conventional set-up.

Here are a few examples of the reverse band set-up.

6

How to determine the amount of band tension
The band resistance should be so high that your sticking point moves up to the top half
of the movement. This generally requires a band resistance in the top position that is
equal to at least 30% of the barbell weight and sometimes over 50%. That means if you
press with 100 kg, the band tension should be 30-50+ kg in the top position. You don't
have to do any calculations though: just put on the bands and see how the exercise
feels.
• If you feel a good amount of resistance throughout the entire movement, you
know the bands are set up correctly.
• If you don't feel any resistance in the bottom anymore but it's extremely difficult
to lock out the weight at the top, you should reduce the band tension.
• If you barely feel the presence of the bands, you should increase the band
tension.

Once you've found a good set-up, you can generally stick to this set-up for many weeks.
You don't have to micromanage the band resistance every session.
headings: How to determine the amount of band tension

pages.text:
7

Squats
You can generally use a similar set-up and band resistance you found for the bench
press for squats. The bands should be attached directly below or above your midfoot
and the bar. Beware that the conventional set-up can make racking and unracking the
bar difficult, so when in doubt, go with less band tension than may be ideal from a
performance point of view.

Here are some examples.
• Reverse band front squats
• Low bar squats with bands

all_headings: How to determine the amount of band tension
